_meta.stack: torch 2.11.0+cu126, transformers 5.4.0, flash-attn 2.8.3.post1 (inference-only build), bitsandbytes 0.49.2, Python 3.11, Windows 11
_meta.prompt: Lily math word problem (~45 natural tokens; --ctx pads with filler)

short_ctx_quant_attn_matrix._description: Natural prompt (~45 tokens), 128 new tokens. Isolates quant vs attention backend.
short_ctx_quant_attn_matrix.verdict: FA2 doesn't matter at short context regardless of quant. bf16 is 35% faster than bnb-4bit (4.1 vs 3.0 tok/s) — the ~90 ms/tok overhead is bnb dequant per forward.

long_ctx_fa2_sweep_bf16._description: Synthetic prompt padded with filler tokens, 64 new tokens. Shows where FA2 actually pays off.

cells.status: degraded — overcommitted into WDDM shared memory
OOM: torch.OutOfMemoryError in sdpa_attention_forward (PyTorch had overcommitted to 24.65 GiB via WDDM before hitting a wall)

long_ctx_fa2_sweep_bf16.verdict: FA2 is a long-context ENABLER, not a tok/s booster. SDPA falls off a cliff at ctx=4096 (27× per-token slowdown via WDDM paging) and OOMs entirely at ctx=8192. FA2 degrades gracefully (4.1 → 3.8 → 3.4 tok/s at 1K → 4K → 8K) with VRAM staying well under the 12 GB card budget.

overall_conclusions: FA2 install was worth doing — but for long-context survival, not short-decode tok/s.
Original SDPA-math-fallback hypothesis was wrong for short-context bnb-4bit Qwen3-4B. Confirmed by 4-cell quant×attn matrix.
bnb 4-bit dequant adds ~90 ms/tok of overhead vs bf16 (336.7 → 246.9 ms/tok). To beat that floor, look at GPTQ-Marlin (fused dequant+matmul) or GGUF/llama.cpp.
On 12 GB VRAM, Qwen3-4B in bf16 ABSOLUTELY requires FA2 above ctx ≈ 2K. Without it, the SDPA path either pages through WDDM shared system memory (catastrophic latency) or OOMs.
